So you guys, if you watch the analytics session, I basically introduced this idea that we need to try to choose one or two, really preferably one key metric to evaluate if a campaign is working or not. And if that one metric looks good, great. If that one metric does not look good, then it's time to go in and do a bit of deeper digging, looking at other things. And I have three main categories of ad type. And the idea is that every ad has a different job. There are some ads that are just designed to get awareness. Those tend to be around video views or getting people to sign up through Messenger, but you may not be using those kind of ads in your business. And that's perfectly fine. I just wanted people to know that it is acceptable and okay to have some ads that are optimized for video view. But not everybody is at that stage. So if you don't have a campaign that is leveraging video views right now, you don't have to have one. That's not a requirement to have a successful winning Facebook ad account. It's just a great way to scale up and build trust as you establish your core base of successfully converting campaigns. So that was one question I saw in this document. I don't run through all these, but just wanted to touch on that. We also have engagement. So these campaigns tend to be focused on lead generation. So that's why there's this conversion tracking campaign. And these awareness engagement and conversion kind of make up the three big buckets of campaign types. You're going to have ads that are designed to

## [06:05 - 09:08]

let people know your brand exists. You're going to have ads that are designed to warm people up, in this case, getting them to opt in. And so these are ones where you want to be looking at the cost per acquisition revenue per visitor and ROI. The cost per acquisition is the key number that I usually look at for conversion campaigns. So big takeaways are you don't need to have all of these. What you want to remember is you're creating a campaign. Stop and think about what the purpose of that campaign is. ## [21:11 - 24:13]

and getting a lot of leads, but those leads are going to naturally drop off a little bit and be slightly less engaged as more time passes. The best way to run in a one time offer to lead generation is right after you finish delivering whatever value you've offered them. So when we run this for a train my traffic person, for example, we start making the offer after we complete whatever the course is we were using to build awareness interest. We start promoting the offer in the email where we deliver that swipe file to them because they've gotten the thing we promised them. So you need to fulfill the promise, but then immediately transition into the one time offer. You don't need to wait, because waiting really just means people's interest and attention is flagging. ## [27:18 - 30:17]

and try to separate the two because the campaign you're running is to generate email leads. It is not to drive the sale of your one-time offer. And the problem is it sounds like you're trying to evaluate the success of your Facebook ad campaign based on this thing that happens 90 days after it, and that's just going to be impossible to do. So what you need to do is we need to figure out what a good cost per lead is for your business and then start evaluating the Facebook campaign's performance based on whether or not you're hitting that cost per lead goal. So you want to be looking for the metrics from your Facebook campaign, how much you spend each week, how many clicks you're getting, and how many leads you're generating so we can figure out what your cost per lead is. So each week you'd go in and you'd plug in, you know, you spent $1,000 here. You can do impressions as well. You can do clicks, put all that in, and you know, we say you spend $1,000 and you got 300 leads. So you're paying $3.33 per lead right now. So that is the thing you want to be keeping an eye on to evaluate your Facebook campaign performance. So that is how we need to think about the Facebook campaign. And on the other side of it, separately from this Facebook campaign, we need to think about how to basically evaluate the success. So what I like to do, for example, with this funnel, I don't really look at what's happening in Facebook to determine if this funnel is succeeding or failing. Instead, I figure out from the people who opted in to this, how do we do, how many of them go on to buy the SEM 2.0 for $500 off? That is how I'm measuring success there. And I want there to be a conversion rate of about 4% from lead to buyer. And I know we have a conversion rate of 4% from lead to buyer, we can pay about $4.50 per lead. So I'm only judging my Facebook success on whether or not I'm getting those leads for $4.50 or less. I'm then evaluating my sales funnel performance, not evaluating the Facebook campaign, evaluating the sales funnel performance by how well I can convert those new leads into buyers. I'm going to change who I'm targeting with the Facebook lead stuff because Facebook ads do affect the ultimate sale. But since there's a big separation between the two, especially for you, 90 days, that's a really long time. We need to cut that down. But that is kind of a thing we need to think about them as two separate pieces. So you want to figure out what your Facebook success metric needs to be based on how well you can convert people into buyers and then evaluate Facebook against that specific number. and you making an offer to them, you are never going to have that funnel self-liquidate. Like, I don't want to be doom and gloom guide, but that is a very, very long creative time. ## [48:40 - 51:41]

actually just going to get harder because of some changes in the industry that are going to reduce the ability to cookie site visitors. So most of you guys probably know about the GDPR. We just had our first sort of incidents of a U.S. equivalent called the CCPA, the California Consumer Privacy Act, which means that customers have the right to ask you to not track them essentially or delete data that you've already tracked. And the other big change this year is that Apple rolled out an update to Safari, where basically after one day, your Facebook tracking cookies get wiped off of somebody's browser on Safari. And Safari represents like 60% of U.S. mobile site visitors. So tracking an ads manager only to understand this longer sales cycle is not going to be possible. I talked a little bit in the training that we did or that I did that you guys watched the recording of about this thing called the halo effect. This idea that there are improvements to your performance across the board that come from running Facebook ads that are impossible to directly tie to the Facebook ads. And that is just the nature of the business, right? Because if you run Facebook ads, there are going to be people that go look up your brand URL on Google, you know, and then they click into your site through there, or there's going to be people that type the URL you put in the ad directly into their browser rather than clicking the ad. So there's always going to be a lift that you see from spin that you can't directly tie back to your Facebook ads, which is a bummer because we are all used to being able to tie, you know, $1 spent to $1 earned. So that's like the traditional perspective that you wanted is like, oh man, what a bummer that I can't track this. But in another sense, it's really awesome that we can still track some performance directly and, you know, evaluate and optimize for that. And then there's all this other great stuff that's happening in addition. So I think it's still worth and good to optimize your Facebook ads to perform against specific metrics. But I also think that we need to just be willing to allocate small chunks of our budget to spending on awareness, things like that. So I would say this is where we talk about the awareness stuff, right? Basically, we're not going to be able to go in and perfectly tie the money that you're spending on driving awareness to the sales. But we can evaluate what ads are better at getting more awareness. So we want to get the most awareness possible for your business for the best price. So we need to kind of decouple the two things in our brain. every month on awareness. And I can directly trace that back to money I made. No, if you try to do that, you're not going to be able to do it. It's just not possible anymore.
